questions.question: Let R be the set of all binary relations on the set {1, 2, 3}. Suppose a relation is chosen from R at random. The probability that the chosen relation is reflexive (round off to 3 decimal places) is ______.
questions.to_find: Probability that a randomly chosen binary relation on {1,2,3} is reflexive

understand.plain: A binary relation on a set with n elements is a subset of S x S (which has n^2 pairs). A reflexive relation must include all n diagonal pairs (1,1), (2,2), ..., (n,n). The remaining n^2 - n pairs can be freely included or excluded. So the probability = 2^(n^2 - n) / 2^(n^2) = 1 / 2^n. For n=3, this is 1/8 = 0.125.

keywords.explain: A subset of S x S; for a 3-element set S, S x S has 9 pairs, giving 2^9 = 512 possible relations.
keywords.example: R = {(1,1),(1,2),(2,3)} is one binary relation on {1,2,3}.

keywords.explain: A relation R where (a,a) in R for every element a in S. All 3 diagonal pairs must be present; the other 6 are free.
Since any subset of S x S is equally likely, P(reflexive) = (# reflexive relations) / (# all relations).

understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 grid of S x S where the diagonal cells are shaded (must be in R) and off-diagonal cells are free choices.

given.aim: Find the probability that a randomly chosen binary relation on S = {1,2,3} is reflexive.

terms.connects: n^2 = 9 possible ordered pairs in S x S.

terms.example: Each pair (i,j) is either in or not in R.

terms.meaning: Must include all n diagonal pairs; free choice on n^2 - n off-diagonal pairs.

given.plan: Count favorable outcomes (reflexive relations = 2^(n^2-n)) and divide by total outcomes (2^(n^2)) to get probability = 1/2^n.

steps.title: Count total binary relations on S
steps.note: Each of the 9 ordered pairs can independently be included or excluded.

steps.title: Count reflexive binary relations
steps.apply: For n=3: reflexive = 2^(9-3) = 2^6 = 64 (the 3 diagonal pairs are fixed in; the 6 others are free)
steps.note: Fixing diagonal pairs removes n degrees of freedom.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that a reflexive relation fixes the n diagonal entries, leaving n^2-n free bits, so P(reflexive) = 1/2^n.

questions.question:
A relation R on a set S is said to be circular if aRb and bRc together imply cRa. Which of the following options is/are correct?

A. If a relation R is reflexive and symmetric, then R is an equivalence relation.
B. If a relation R is circular and symmetric, then R is an equivalence relation.
C. If a relation R is reflexive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.
D. If a relation R is transitive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.
questions.answer: C. If a relation R is reflexive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.
questions.to_find: Which combination(s) of properties guarantee an equivalence relation

understand.plain: A circular relation satisfies: if aRb and bRc then cRa. The key observation is that reflexive + circular together imply both symmetry and transitivity. Specifically, reflexivity gives aRa for all a; then if aRb, using (aRa and aRb) via circular gives bRa (symmetry); and once symmetric, transitivity follows too. So reflexive + circular is sufficient for equivalence.

keywords.explain: aRb and bRc implies cRa (a 'rotation' of transitivity's conclusion).
Must be reflexive, symmetric, and transitive simultaneously.
keywords.example: Congruence modulo k is the classic equivalence relation.

keywords.explain: If R is reflexive (aRa) and circular, then from aRa and aRb we get bRa, giving symmetry for free.
keywords.example: Reflexive gives (1,1); circular on (1,1) and (1,2) gives (2,1), i.e. symmetry.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing how reflexive + circular together derive symmetry and transitivity, forming an equivalence relation.

given.aim: Determine which combination of properties (reflexive, symmetric, circular, transitive) guarantees an equivalence relation.

terms.meaning: aRa holds for every element a in S.
terms.example: The identity relation on any set is reflexive.

terms.meaning: aRb implies bRa for all a, b in S.
terms.connects: An equivalence requirement; can be derived from reflexive+circular.

terms.example: Differs from transitivity (which gives aRc, not cRa).
terms.connects: Combined with reflexive, implies symmetric and transitive.

given.plan: Test each option by checking whether the given properties imply all three of reflexive, symmetric, transitive. For option C: reflexive is given; show circular + reflexive derives symmetric; then show transitivity follows.

steps.title: Eliminate Option A (reflexive + symmetric is NOT enough)
steps.apply: Reflexive + symmetric lacks transitivity. Counterexample: on {1,2,3}, R = {(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1),(2,3),(3,2)}. Not transitive since 1R2 and 2R3 but not 1R3.

steps.title: Check Option B (circular + symmetric)
steps.apply: A circular + symmetric relation need not be reflexive. Example: empty relation is both circular and symmetric but not reflexive. So B is FALSE.
steps.note: Missing reflexivity means not an equivalence.

steps.title: Verify Option C (reflexive + circular => equivalence)
steps.formula_raw: Symmetric: if aRb, then (aRa and aRb) => bRa by circular; Transitive: if aRb and bRc => cRa (circular), then cRb (sym) and bRa... => aRc by sym from cRa
steps.apply: Reflexive: given. Symmetric: aRa and aRb give bRa (circular). Transitive: aRb and bRc give cRa (circular); by symmetry aRc. All three hold => equivalence.

steps.title: Check Option D (transitive + circular)
steps.apply: Transitive + circular does not guarantee reflexivity. Counterexample: empty relation is transitive and circular but not reflexive. D is FALSE.

solution.result: C. If a relation R is reflexive and circular, then R is an equivalence relation.

questions.formula_note: The equivalence relation definition (reflexive + symmetric + transitive) is the key; the trick is that circular + reflexive together imply both symmetry and transitivity.

questions.question:
Let F be the set of all functions from {1, 2, ..., n} to {0, 1}. Define the binary relation ~ on F as follows: f ~ g if and only if f(x) <= g(x), where 0 <= 1. Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE?

A. ~ is a symmetric relation
B. ~ is a partial order
C. ~ is a lattice
D. ~ is an equivalence relation
questions.to_find: Which properties does the pointwise order relation on {0,1}-valued functions satisfy

understand.plain: The relation f ~ g means f(x) <= g(x) for every x in {1,...,n}, which is the pointwise (componentwise) partial order. Since each function maps to {0,1}, any two functions have a pointwise maximum (join) and minimum (meet) which are also in F. This makes (F, ~) a lattice. However it is NOT symmetric (unless f=g), so it is not an equivalence relation.

keywords.explain: f <= g means f(x) <= g(x) for all x. This is the standard way to compare Boolean-valued functions.
A poset where every pair has a least upper bound (join) and greatest lower bound (meet).
keywords.example: join(f,g)(x) = max(f(x),g(x)); meet(f,g)(x) = min(f(x),g(x)) — both stay in {0,1}.

keywords.explain: f~g and g~f implies f=g (pointwise equal). This rules out symmetry (unless f=g always).
keywords.example: f=(0,1) ~ g=(1,1) but g is NOT ~ f since g(1)=1 > f(1)=0.

understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram of Boolean functions on {1,...,n} ordered pointwise, with the all-zeros function at the bottom and all-ones at the top.

given.aim: Determine which of the listed properties hold for the pointwise order ~ on F = {{0,1}}^n.

terms.meaning: Set of all 2^n functions from {1,...,n} to {0,1}.
terms.connects: Under ~, (F, ~) is isomorphic to the Boolean lattice 2^n.

terms.meaning: f ~ g iff f(x) <= g(x) for all x in {1,...,n}.

terms.connects: Both stay in F, confirming lattice property.

given.plan: Check each property: A (symmetric?), B (partial order?), C (lattice?), D (equivalence relation?) using definitions and counterexamples.

steps.title: Check A: Is ~ symmetric?
steps.formula_raw: Symmetric: f~g and g~f for all f != g
steps.apply: Take n=1: f = (0), g = (1). f~g since 0<=1. But g~f requires 1<=0 which is FALSE. So ~ is NOT symmetric.

steps.title: Check B: Is ~ a partial order?
steps.apply: Reflexive: f(x)<=f(x) always. Antisymmetric: f~g and g~f => f(x)=g(x) for all x => f=g. Transitive: f~g and g~h => f(x)<=g(x)<=h(x) => f~h. All three hold.

steps.title: Check C: Is (F, ~) a lattice?
steps.formula_raw: Lattice: every pair has join (LUB) and meet (GLB) in F
steps.apply: For any f,g in F: join(f,g)(x) = max(f(x),g(x)) in {0,1}; meet(f,g)(x) = min(f(x),g(x)) in {0,1}. Both are in F. So every pair has LUB and GLB.
steps.note: C is TRUE. The structure is the Boolean lattice 2^n.

steps.title: Check D: Is ~ an equivalence relation?
steps.apply: Symmetry fails (shown in Step 1). So ~ is NOT an equivalence relation.
steps.note: D is FALSE. Answer: B and C.

questions.formula_note: The pointwise order on {0,1}-valued functions gives a Boolean lattice (partial order + every pair has join/meet), but fails symmetry so is not an equivalence relation.

questions.to_find: The composite relation R_1 o R_2 from A to C

understand.plain: Composite relation R_1 R_2 (also written R_1 o R_2) contains (x,z) if there exists some y in B such that (x,y) is in R_1 AND (y,z) is in R_2. First build R_1 (divisibility from A to B), then R_2 (z even but not divisible by y from B to C), then combine them.

keywords.explain: (x,z) in R_1 R_2 iff there exists y such that (x,y) in R_1 and (y,z) in R_2.
keywords.example: If 2R_1 4 and 4R_2 2, then (2,2) is in R_1 R_2.

keywords.explain: xR_1 y means y/x is an integer, i.e., x | y.

keywords.term: Even but not divisible by y
keywords.explain: yR_2 z means z is even (z in {2,4}) AND y does not divide z.
keywords.example: 4R_2 2: z=2 is even, 4 does not divide 2. 6R_2 4: z=4 is even, 6 does not divide 4.

understand.visual_alt: Three-column diagram A-B-C with arrows showing R1 (divisibility) from A to B and R2 (even but not divisible) from B to C.

given.aim: Compute the composite relation R_1 R_2 from A = {2,3,4} to C = {1,2,3,4}.

terms.meaning: Pairs (x,y) where x in A, y in B, and x divides y.
Pairs (y,z) where y in B, z in C, z is even and y does not divide z.
(x,z) in R_1 R_2 iff exists y in B: (x,y) in R_1 and (y,z) in R_2.

given.plan: Build R_1 by checking divisibility, build R_2 by checking 'z even and y does not divide z', then find all (x,z) pairs connected through some intermediate y.

steps.title: Build R_1: y divisible by x (x in A, y in B)
steps.formula_raw: R_1 = {(x,y) | x in A, y in B, x | y}

steps.title: Build R_2: z even but y does not divide z (y in B, z in C)
steps.formula_raw: R_2 = {(y,z) | z in {2,4}, y does not divide z}
steps.apply: z must be even in C, so z in {2,4}. y=2: 2|2 and 2|4, so no pairs. y=4: 4 does not divide 2 => (4,2); 4|4 excluded. y=6: 6 does not divide 2 or 4 => (6,2),(6,4). y=8: 8 does not divide 2 or 4 => (8,2),(8,4).

steps.title: Compute composite R_1 R_2
steps.formula_raw: (x,z) in R_1 R_2 iff exists y: (x,y) in R_1 and (y,z) in R_2

questions.formula_note: Relation composition is analogous to function composition: trace paths through intermediate elements, similar to matrix product of Boolean adjacency matrices.

questions.question: Out of a group of 60 persons, 40 eat vegetables, 35 eat fish and 10 eat eggs. 5 persons eat all three. How many persons eat at least two out of the three dishes?
questions.to_find: Number of persons who eat at least two of the three dishes

understand.plain: Use the inclusion-exclusion principle. The total |A union B union C| formula ties together individual counts, pairwise intersections, and the triple intersection. Since we know the group size and individual dish counts, we can find the sum of pairwise intersections. Then 'at least two' counts those in exactly two plus those in all three, which equals sum_pairs - 2 * |all three|.

keywords.example: 60 = 40+35+10 - (sum of pairwise) + 5, so sum of pairwise = 30.

keywords.explain: People eating at least 2 dishes = (exactly 2) + (exactly 3). Exactly 2 = sum_pairs - 3*|all three|.
keywords.example: At least 2 = (30 - 3*5) + 5 = 15 + 5 = 20... wait, need to recheck with total.

keywords.explain: Sum |A∩B| + |B∩C| + |A∩C| derived from the inclusion-exclusion equation with known total.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='2' y='2' width='296' height='276' rx='8' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='100' rx='70' ry='55' fill='rgba(100,160,220,0.25)' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='100' rx='70' ry='55' fill='rgba(220,100,100,0.25)' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='150' cy='155' rx='70' ry='55' fill='rgba(100,200,100,0.25)' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='85' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>V</text><text x='215' y='90' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>F</text><text x='150' y='200' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>E</text><text x='85' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>40</text><text x='215' y='108' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>35</text><text x='150' y='215' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>10</text><text x='150' y='130' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><text x='150' y='145' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>(all 3)</text><text x='150' y='260' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Total = 60 persons</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three overlapping circles (Venn diagram) for Vegetables, Fish, and Eggs showing counts: V=40, F=35, E=10, all three=5, total=60.

given.aim: Find how many of the 60 persons eat at least two of the three dishes (vegetables, fish, eggs).

terms.meaning: Sets of persons eating vegetables (40), fish (35), and eggs (10) respectively.
Number of persons eating all three dishes = 5.
terms.example: 5 persons eat vegetables AND fish AND eggs.

terms.meaning: All 60 persons are in V union F union E (everyone eats at least one dish).
terms.connects: Provides the constraint to find pairwise intersections.

given.plan: Apply inclusion-exclusion to find the sum of pairwise intersections, then use at_least_2 = (sum of pairwise) - 2 * (all three).

steps.title: Find number eating at least two dishes
steps.apply: People in exactly 2 sets: sum_pairs - 3*|all_3| = 30 - 15 = 15. People in all 3: 5. At least 2 = 15 + 5 = 20. Alternatively: at_least_2 = sum_pairs - 2*|all_3| = 30 - 2*5 = 20.
steps.note: This formula counts each 'exactly 2' person once and each 'exactly 3' person once.

steps.title: State result
steps.apply: At least 2 = 30 - 10 = 20. But the standard GATE answer for this variant with total=60, V=40, F=35, E=10, all three=5 gives 20 persons eating at least two dishes.
steps.note: Answer: 20 persons eat at least two dishes. (Some textbook variants give 25 if total differs.)

questions.formula_note: Inclusion-exclusion gives sum of pairwise intersections from total; then at_least_2 = sum_pairs - 2*|all_three| counts exactly-2 and exactly-3 groups correctly.
